annotations.label: Four color theorem

anchor.snippet: no more than four colors are required to color the regions of any map so that no two adjacent regions have the same color

annotations.note: Graph coloring infrastructure (Colorable, chromaticNumber) exists but planar graphs and the four color theorem itself are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Five color theorem

anchor.snippet: stronger version of the five color theorem

annotations.note: No notion of planar graphs nor the five color theorem appears in Mathlib (grep for 'five color' yields nothing).

annotations.label: Graph-theoretic formulation (chromatic number)

anchor.snippet: In graph-theoretic terms, the theorem states that for a loopless planar graph

annotations.note: SimpleGraph.chromaticNumber is defined but planarity is not, so the statement χ(G) ≤ 4 for planar G cannot be stated.

annotations.label: Adjacency of regions

anchor.snippet: regions are adjacent if they share a boundary segment

annotations.note: Abstract graph adjacency (SimpleGraph.Adj) exists, but geometric adjacency of map regions sharing a boundary segment is not formalized.

annotations.label: Same-country map requiring five colors

anchor.snippet: In this map, the two regions labeled A belong to the same country.

annotations.note: An illustrative map example with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Map represented as planar graph

anchor.snippet: an undirected graph that has a vertex for each region and an edge for every pair of regions that share a boundary segment

annotations.note: Mathlib has SimpleGraph but no construction of a graph from a planar map's regions.

annotations.label: Every planar graph is four-colorable

anchor.snippet: every planar graph is four-colorable

annotations.note: This is the four color theorem, which is not in Mathlib, and planar graphs are not defined.

annotations.label: De Morgan's four-areas postulate

anchor.snippet: four areas cannot each have common boundary with all the other three without inclosure

annotations.note: No formalization of mutual boundary-sharing of planar regions exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heawood: five color theorem and genus generalization

anchor.snippet: Heawood proved the five color theorem and generalized the four color conjecture to surfaces of arbitrary genus

annotations.note: Neither the five color theorem nor coloring on surfaces of arbitrary genus is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tait's snark equivalence

anchor.snippet: the four color theorem is equivalent to the statement that a certain type of graph

annotations.note: Edge 3-colorings of cubic planar graphs and Tait's equivalence are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hadwiger conjecture

anchor.snippet: Hugo Hadwiger formulated the Hadwiger conjecture

annotations.note: Grep for 'Hadwiger' in Mathlib returns no results; graph minors and the conjecture are absent.

annotations.label: Minimal counterexample requiring five colors

anchor.snippet: there would be at least one map with the smallest possible number of regions that requires five colors

annotations.note: The minimal-counterexample setup of the four color proof is not formalized.

annotations.label: Unavoidable set

anchor.snippet: An unavoidable set is a set of configurations such that every map that satisfies some necessary conditions

annotations.note: The notion of an unavoidable set of configurations is specific to the four color proof and absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reducible configuration

anchor.snippet: A reducible configuration is an arrangement of countries that cannot occur in a minimal counterexample.

annotations.note: Reducible configurations are not defined anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quadratic-time 4-coloring algorithm

anchor.snippet: created a quadratic-time algorithm

annotations.note: No four-coloring algorithm or its complexity analysis exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Snark conjecture proof

anchor.snippet: announced an alternative proof, by proving the snark conjecture

annotations.note: Snarks and the snark conjecture are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reduction to triangulated graphs

anchor.snippet: it suffices to prove the four color theorem for triangulated graphs to prove it for all planar graphs

annotations.note: Triangulated planar graphs and this reduction are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Edge-face count relation 2e = 3f

anchor.snippet: Since each region is triangular and each edge is shared by two regions

annotations.note: Faces of planar embeddings and Euler-formula counting relations for planar graphs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Degree of a vertex

anchor.snippet: the degree of a vertex is the number of edges abutting it

annotations.note: SimpleGraph.degree is defined as the number of vertices adjacent to v (= edges abutting it), matching the definition exactly.

annotations.label: Existence of a vertex of degree 5 or less

anchor.snippet: this demonstrates that there is at least one vertex of degree 5 or less

annotations.note: This minimum-degree fact relies on planarity/Euler's formula, neither of which is formalized for graphs in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence of minimal 5-chromatic graph

anchor.snippet: If there is a graph requiring 5 colors, then there is a minimal such graph, where removing any vertex makes it four-colorable.

annotations.note: Mathlib defines chromaticNumber but has no notion of color-critical/minimal counterexample graphs.

annotations.label: No vertex of degree 3 or less in minimal graph

anchor.snippet: Then G cannot have a vertex of degree 3 or less

annotations.note: This step of the four color proof about critical graphs is not formalized.

annotations.label: No vertex of degree 4 in minimal graph

anchor.snippet: Kempe also showed correctly that G can have no vertex of degree 4.

annotations.note: The Kempe-chain argument excluding degree-4 vertices is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kempe chain

anchor.snippet: Such a path is called a Kempe chain .

annotations.note: Kempe chains (two-color alternating paths) are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Configuration

anchor.snippet: considering configurations , which are connected subgraphs of G with the degree of each vertex (in G) specified

annotations.note: The four color proof's notion of a configuration is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reducible configuration (general)

anchor.snippet: A configuration for which this is possible is called a reducible configuration .

annotations.note: Reducibility of configurations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unavoidable set (general)

anchor.snippet: If at least one of a set of configurations must occur somewhere in G, that set is called unavoidable .

annotations.note: Unavoidable sets of configurations are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ring and k-ring configuration

anchor.snippet: These vertices form the ring of the configuration; a configuration with k vertices in its ring is a k -ring configuration

annotations.note: The ring of a configuration and k-ring terminology are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Initially good coloring

anchor.snippet: any coloring that can be extended without modification to a coloring of the configuration is called initially good

annotations.note: The 'initially good' coloring notion from the four color proof is not formalized.

annotations.label: Method of discharging

anchor.snippet: The primary method used to discover such a set is the method of discharging .

annotations.note: The discharging method is not formalized anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Immersion reducibility

anchor.snippet: A technical detail not discussed here but required to complete the proof is immersion reducibility .

annotations.note: Immersion reducibility is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Color restriction is not transitive

anchor.snippet: the color restriction is not transitive

annotations.note: This informal observation about coloring constraints has no corresponding Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: NP-completeness of three-coloring planar maps

anchor.snippet: it is NP-complete in complexity to decide whether an arbitrary planar map can be colored with just three colors

annotations.note: Computational complexity classes like NP-completeness are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three-coloring of cubic maps

anchor.snippet: A cubic map can be colored with only three colors if and only if each interior region has an even number of neighboring regions.

annotations.note: This three-colorability criterion for cubic planar maps is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Missouri and Nevada neighbor parity

anchor.snippet: landlocked Missouri (MO) has eight neighbors

annotations.note: A concrete geographic example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Four color theorem for infinite planar graphs

anchor.snippet: The four color theorem applies not only to finite planar graphs, but also to infinite graphs that can be drawn without crossings in the plane

annotations.note: This depends on both the (absent) four color theorem and the (absent) notion of infinite planar graphs.

annotations.label: De Bruijn–Erdős theorem

anchor.snippet: the De Bruijn–Erdős theorem stating that, if every finite subgraph of an infinite graph is k -colorable, then the whole graph is also k -colorable

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the compactness result that if every finite subgraph maps to a finite graph F then so does G; taking F a complete graph on k colors yields De Bruijn–Erdős, so this generalizes the k-colorability statement.

annotations.label: Colors needed depend on Euler characteristic

anchor.snippet: the maximum number p of colors needed depends on the Euler characteristic χ of the surface

annotations.note: Coloring maps on surfaces and the Heawood number formula are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the maximum number p of colors needed depends on the Euler characteristic χ of the surface

annotations.label: Orientable surface formula in terms of genus

anchor.snippet: this implies that p can be given in terms of the genus g of the surface

annotations.note: No formalization of map coloring numbers in terms of surface genus exists in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: this implies that p can be given in terms of the genus g of the surface

annotations.label: Heawood conjecture

anchor.snippet: The top formula, the Heawood conjecture , was proposed by P. J. Heawood in 1890

annotations.note: The Heawood conjecture/Ringel–Youngs theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Klein bottle requires only six colors

anchor.snippet: but requires only six colors, as shown by Philip Franklin in 1934

annotations.note: Map coloring on the Klein bottle is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Torus requires seven colors

anchor.snippet: so no more than seven colors are required to color any map on a torus

annotations.note: Map coloring on the torus is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Möbius strip and 1-planar graphs require six colors

anchor.snippet: A Möbius strip requires six colors

annotations.note: Coloring on the Möbius strip and 1-planar graphs is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Real projective plane requires six colors

anchor.snippet: so no more than six colors are required

annotations.note: Map coloring on the real projective plane is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Earth–Moon problem chromatic bounds

anchor.snippet: the chromatic number can be at least 9 and is at most 12

annotations.note: The Earth–Moon (biplanar graph) coloring bounds are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Folded rods needing arbitrarily many colors

anchor.snippet: By using a set of n folded rods, one can arrange that every rod touches every other rod.

annotations.note: This 3D arrangement example forcing arbitrarily many colors has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Axis-parallel cuboids need unbounded colors

anchor.snippet: Even for axis-parallel cuboids

annotations.note: Coloring of axis-parallel cuboid arrangements is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bar-Natan's equivalent statement

anchor.section: Relation to other areas of mathematics
anchor.snippet: Dror Bar-Natan gave a statement concerning Lie algebras and Vassiliev invariants which is equivalent to the four color theorem.

annotations.note: Bar-Natan's Lie-algebra/Vassiliev-invariant reformulation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Map of Europe not four-colorable with water color

anchor.snippet: Then the map of Europe itself is not four-colorable.

annotations.note: An applied illustrative example with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.
